plant_profile.history: Unique History: Classified under the 'Deepaniya' (appetizer) and 'Shothahara' (anti-inflammatory) groups in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita. It is one of the pillars of Ayurvedic clinical pharmacology for treating cold and wet diseases. Its name 'Chitrak' translates to 'Fire-like' or 'variegated'. Physical Characteristics: A perennial, subscandent shrub growing up to 1-2 meters in height, with a spreading crown and thin, greyish stems. It features ovate-elliptic, dark-green leaves and small, white flowers arranged in terminal spikes (catkins). The root is thick, fleshy, and has a brownish bark with a pungent, biting taste. It is found throughout India and tropical South Asia as a wild or cultivated plant. Specific Chemical Compounds: Primarily contains Plumbagin (naphthoquinone), Chitranone, and Droserone. The presence of plumbagin is crucial for its bio-enhancing, anti-microbial, and anti-tubercular action on the immune system. Strict Standardizations: The root is standardized in API based on macroscopic features (tough bark, extremely pungent taste). Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<3%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>5%), and water-soluble extractive (>15%). HPTLC quantification is used to monitor plumbagin content. Note: It requires purification (Shodhana) with lime water before use. Purification Process (Shodhana): The roots are traditionally soaked in lime water (Chuna-jala) for several days followed by washing with hot water to remove excessive irritant properties before medicinal use.

ayurvedic_properties.dosha_modulation: Prominently pacifies Vata and Kapha doshas. Due to its Katu (pungent) Rasa and Atyanta-Ushna (extremely hot) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Deepana' (appetizer) and 'Pachana' (digestive) therapies, clearing the cold and heavy qualities of Kapha. It may significantly aggravate Pitta if used in excess.

medicinal_uses.digestive_fire_igniter: Known as 'Agni-Sama' (equal to fire). It is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for all conditions of low digestive fire (Mandagni). Its Tikshna (sharp) and Atyanta-Ushna (extremely hot) nature helps in instantaneously igniting the 'Jatharagni' and clearing deep-seated 'Ama' (toxins). WHO-aligned research supports its prokinetic and digestive effects.
medicinal_uses.hemorrhoids_specialist: Acts as a powerful 'Arshoghna' (anti-piles) agent. It helps in reducing the size of hemorrhoidal masses and improving the vascular tone of the rectal area through its systemic Vata-pacifying and digestive effects.
medicinal_uses.metabolic_support_and_obesity: Helps in reducing excessive body fat and Meda (fatty tissue) by stimulating the 'Agni' (metabolism) and inhibiting the synthesis of fatty acids. It is often combined with black pepper and ginger for enhancing metabolism.
medicinal_uses.abdominal_pain_and_colic: Its 'Shulaghna' (analgesic) property provides substantial relief from abdominal cramps and flatulence-related pain. It is often used in combination with Hingu (Asafoetida) for gastrointestinal relief.
medicinal_uses.anti_inflammatory_and_analgesic: Systemically used for conditions like rheumatoid arthritis (Amavata) and chronic joint pain by clearing blocked Vata pathways and reducing local swelling through its hot-sharp property.
medicinal_uses.skin_and_vitiligo_support: Its 'Kusthaghna' (remedy for skin) property help in managing chronic skin conditions like vitiligo by stimulating the local blood flow and melanin production when applied topically in purified forms.
medicinal_uses.phytochemical_profile: Rich in Plumbagin (a key naphthoquinone), Chitranone, Zeylanone, and various sterols that provide its multifaceted medicinal benefits, particularly its potent anti-microbial and anti-carcinogenic properties.

preparation_methods.purified_root_powder__churna_: 250 mg to 500 mg of fine powder taken twice daily with buttermilk (which acts as a cooling Anupana).
preparation_methods.chitrakadi_vati: Classical tablet form used for indigestion and low appetite; taken (250-500mg) before or after meals.
preparation_methods.root_decoction__kwatha_: 5g of dried root boiled in 100ml water reduced to 25ml for chronic digestive and metabolic congestion.
preparation_methods.medicated_ghee: The purified root is processed with cow's ghee to treat chronic Vata and digestive issues through its 'Pachana' (digestive) quality.
preparation_methods.external_paste: Ground purified root or paste applied directly to skin rashes (vitiligo) topically under expert guidance to stimulate local circulation.

dosage.adults: Powder: 250 mg to 500 mg; Decoction: 10-20 ml; Tablet: 250-500 mg, divided throughout the day before or after meals.
dosage.children: Generally NOT recommended for children unless under strict expert guidance. Smallest doses (25-50mg) used for severe digestive stagnation.
dosage.elderly: Very low doses (125-250mg) are advisable initially, monitoring for any increase in Pitta or stomach burning.

contraindications: Strictly contraindicated in individuals with high Pitta (burning sensation, hyperacidity, stomach ulcers). Not for use during pregnancy as it is a potent uterine stimulant. Avoid in extreme skin sensitivity.
side_effects: Overdose or non-purified use results in stomach burning, vomiting, diarrhea, skin blistering, and excessive thirst.
drug_interactions: Monitor if taken alongside synthetic anti-diabetic or anticoagulant medications as it may potentiate their effects. No severe adverse interactions reported in WHO monographs.
safety.toxicity_notes: GRAS Status: Classified as a safe medicinal herb ONLY when properly purified (Shodhana) and taken in recommended therapeutic dosages. Non-purified roots are toxic and cause blistering.
